content.text:
No, we are **not** sending any emails. Here's the flow:

1. User fills in email + role on the Organization page and clicks "Invite"
2. `authClient.organization.inviteMember()` is called → hits `better-auth`'s `/api/auth/organization/invite-member`
3. An invitation record is created in Postgres
4. The frontend generates a shareable link from the returned invitation ID: `{origin}/invitation/{id}`
5. The user is expected to **copy and share that link manually** — there's no email delivery

The `organization()` plugin in `apps/api/src/auth.ts` is configured without a `sendInvitationEmail` callback, which is what `better-auth` uses to hook into email sending. It just looks like:

```ts
organization({
  allowUserToCreateOrganization: true,
  creatorRole: "owner",
}),
```

To send emails on invite, you'd need to add a `sendInvitationEmail` option there pointing to an email service (Resend, Postmark, etc.).

content.text:
The org resolution priority is (from `upload.ts:116-117` and `project-config.ts`):

1. **`--org` flag** — explicit override at upload time (`rudel upload --org <id>`)
2. **Per-project config** — stored in `~/.rudel/projects.json`, keyed by git remote URL (or absolute path as fallback). Set by running `rudel set-org` in the repo directory.
3. **Fallback: nothing** — `organizationId` is `undefined`, which presumably means the API assigns it to the user's default org (their personal workspace created at signup)

So the flow is:
- Run `rudel set-org` once per repo → picks from your org list interactively → saves to `~/.rudel/projects.json`
- Subsequent `rudel upload` in that repo reads the saved org ID automatically
- The key used is the git remote URL (normalized), so it's portable across machines cloning the same repo

1→# Rudel
     2→
     3→Analytics for [Claude Code](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code). Rudel gives you a dashboard with insights on your coding sessions — token usage, session duration, activity patterns, model usage, and more.
     4→
     5→## Prerequisites
     6→
     7→- [Bun](https://bun.sh) runtime installed
     8→
     9→## Getting Started
    10→
    11→1. Create an account at [app.rudel.ai](https://app.rudel.ai)
    12→2. Install the CLI and connect it to your account:
    13→
    14→```bash
    15→npm install -g rudel
    16→
    17→rudel login     # authenticate via your browser
    18→rudel enable    # auto-upload sessions when Claude Code exits
    19→```
    20→
    21→That's it. Your Claude Code sessions will now be uploaded automatically.
    22→
    23→Already have past sessions? Upload them in one go:
    24→
    25→```bash
    26→rudel upload    # interactive picker for batch upload
    27→```
    28→
    29→See the [CLI documentation](apps/cli/README.md) for all available commands.
    30→
    31→## How It Works
    32→
    33→1. You install the CLI and run `rudel enable`
    34→2. This registers a [Claude Code hook](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks) that runs when a session ends
    35→3. The hook uploads the session transcript to Rudel
    36→4. Transcripts are stored in ClickHouse and processed into analytics
    37→
    38→## What Data Is Collected
    39→
    40→Each uploaded session includes:
    41→
    42→- Session ID & timestamps (start, last interaction)
    43→- User ID & organization ID
    44→- Project path & package name
    45→- Git context (repository, branch, SHA, remote)
    46→- Session transcript (full prompt & response content)
    47→- Sub-agent usage
    48→
    49→## Development
    50→
    51→See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for local setup, development commands, and PR guidelines.
    52→
    53→For self-hosting your own instance, see [docs/self-hosting.md](docs/self-hosting.md).
    54→
    55→## Security
    56→
    57→To report a vulnerability, see [SECURITY.md](SECURITY.md). Do not open public issues for security concerns.
    58→
    59→## License
    60→
    61→[MIT](LICENSE)
    62→

1→# Self-Hosting Rudel
     2→
     3→This guide walks through deploying Rudel using **ObsessionDB** (ClickHouse), **Neon** (Postgres), and **Fly.io** (app server). This is the same stack we use in production.
     4→
     5→> **This is an opinionated guide.** Rudel is a Bun server that needs Postgres and ClickHouse — it can run anywhere those are available. You don't have to use these specific providers. Any Postgres instance, any ClickHouse instance (that supports `SharedReplacingMergeTree` or `ReplacingMergeTree`), and any platform that can run a Bun/Node.js server will work. We use ObsessionDB, Neon, and Fly.io because they all have free tiers and are easy to set up.
     6→
     7→## Services
     8→
     9→| Component | Provider | Free Tier | Purpose |
    10→|-----------|----------|-----------|---------|
    11→| **ClickHouse** | [ObsessionDB](https://obsessiondb.com) | Yes | Session transcript storage and analytics |
    12→| **Postgres** | [Neon](https://neon.tech) | Yes (0.5 GB) | Authentication (users, sessions, accounts) |
    13→| **App Server** | [Fly.io](https://fly.io) | Yes (3 shared VMs) | HTTP API + static frontend serving |
    14→
    15→## 1. Provision ClickHouse (ObsessionDB)
    16→
    17→1. Create an account at [obsessiondb.com](https://obsessiondb.com)
    18→2. Create a new ClickHouse instance
    19→3. Note your connection details:
    20→   - **Host** — the HTTPS endpoint (e.g. `https://your-instance.obsessiondb.com`)
    21→   - **Username** — your username
    22→   - **Password** — your password
    23→4. Apply the schema migration:
    24→
    25→```bash
    26→# From the repo root — set the env vars that chkit reads
    27→CLICKHOUSE_URL=https://your-instance.obsessiondb.com \
    28→CLICKHOUSE_USER=your-username \
    29→REDACTED \
    30→CLICKHOUSE_DB=default \
    31→  bun --bun --cwd packages/ch-schema chkit migrate --apply
    32→```
    33→
    34→The migration creates the `rudel` database, the `claude_sessions` and `session_analytics` tables, and a materialized view that computes analytics on insert. The cloud migration uses `SharedReplacingMergeTree` for `claude_sessions`.
    35→
    36→> **Note**: `CLICKHOUSE_DB` must be set to `default` for the migration because the `rudel` database doesn't exist yet — the migration creates it. `CLICKHOUSE_USER` (not `CLICKHOUSE_USERNAME`) is the env var name that the migration tool expects.
    37→
    38→## 2. Provision Postgres (Neon)
    39→
    40→1. Create an account at [neon.tech](https://neon.tech)
    41→2. Create a new project and database named `rudel`
    42→3. Copy your connection string (looks like `postgres://user:pass@host/rudel?sslmode=require`)
    43→4. Run the Drizzle migrations:
    44→
    45→```bash
    46→PG_CONNECTION_STRING="postgres://user:pass@host/rudel?sslmode=require" \
    47→  bun run --cwd packages/sql-schema migrate
    48→```
    49→
    50→This creates the auth tables (users, sessions, accounts, verification tokens) used by `better-auth`.
    51→
    52→## 3. Deploy to Fly.io
    53→
    54→1. Install the Fly CLI: `curl -L https://fly.io/install.sh | sh`
    55→2. Sign up or log in: `fly auth login`
    56→3. Create a new app:
    57→
    58→```bash
    59→fly launch --name your-app-name --no-deploy
    60→```
    61→
    62→4. Set secrets (the API reads `CLICKHOUSE_USERNAME`, not `CLICKHOUSE_USER`):
    63→
    64→```bash
    65→fly secrets set \
    66→  PG_CONNECTION_STRING="postgres://user:pass@host/rudel?sslmode=require" \
    67→  BETTER_AUTH_SECRET="$(openssl rand -base64 32)" \
    68→  CLICKHOUSE_URL="https://your-instance.obsessiondb.com" \
    69→  CLICKHOUSE_USERNAME="your-username" \
    70→  CLICKHOUSE_PASSWORD="your-password" \
    71→  APP_URL="https://your-app-name.fly.dev" \
    72→  ALLOWED_ORIGIN="https://your-app-name.fly.dev"
    73→```
    74→
    75→5. Deploy (the `Dockerfile` in the repo root handles building the frontend and running the API):
    76→
    77→```bash
    78→fly deploy
    79→```
    80→
    81→6. Verify it's running:
    82→
    83→```bash
    84→curl https://your-app-name.fly.dev/health
    85→```
    86→
    87→### Custom domain
    88→
    89→If you want to use a custom domain instead of `*.fly.dev`:
    90→
    91→```bash
    92→fly certs add your-domain.com
    93→```
    94→
    95→Then update `APP_URL` and `ALLOWED_ORIGIN` to match:
    96→
    97→```bash
    98→fly secrets set \
    99→  APP_URL="https://your-domain.com" \
   100→  ALLOWED_ORIGIN="https://your-domain.com"
   101→```
   102→
   103→## Social Login (Optional)
   104→
   105→To enable GitHub or Google OAuth login, create OAuth apps with the respective providers and set the client credentials:
   106→
   107→```bash
   108→fly secrets set \
   109→  GITHUB_CLIENT_ID="your-client-id" \
   110→  GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET="your-client-secret"
   111→```
   112→
   113→Set the OAuth callback URL to `https://your-domain.com/api/auth/callback/github` (or `/google`).
   114→
   115→Without these, users can still sign up and log in with email/password.
   116→
   117→## Environment Variables Reference
   118→
   119→| Variable | Required | Description |
   120→|----------|----------|-------------|
   121→| `PG_CONNECTION_STRING` | Yes | Postgres connection string |
   122→| `BETTER_AUTH_SECRET` | Yes | Auth secret (`openssl rand -base64 32`) |
   123→| `CLICKHOUSE_URL` | Yes | ClickHouse HTTPS endpoint |
   124→| `CLICKHOUSE_USERNAME` | Yes | ClickHouse username |
   125→| `CLICKHOUSE_PASSWORD` | Yes | ClickHouse password |
   126→| `APP_URL` | Yes | Public URL of the deployed app |
   127→| `ALLOWED_ORIGIN` | Yes | CORS origin (same as `APP_URL` for single-domain) |
   128→| `GITHUB_CLIENT_ID` | No | GitHub OAuth client ID |
   129→| `GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET` | No | GitHub OAuth client secret |
   130→| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` | No | Google OAuth client ID |
   131→| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` | No | Google OAuth client secret |
   132→

1→# Contributing to Rudel
     2→
     3→Thanks for your interest in contributing! This guide covers setup, workflow, and expectations.
     4→
     5→## Prerequisites

cd rudel

- Deploy with `fly deploy`

- Custom domain setup
- Social login (GitHub/Google OAuth)

- `CLICKHOUSE_USERNAME` (required)
- `CLICKHOUSE_PASSWORD` (required)
- `APP_URL` (required) — Public URL
- `ALLOWED_ORIGIN` (required) — CORS origin
- `GITHUB_CLIENT_ID/SECRET` (optional)
- `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID/SECRET` (optional)

---

2. **CONTRIBUTING.md** — Developer setup (local development with Docker, PR guidelines, verification commands)
3. **docs/self-hosting.md** — Deployment guide (provisioning services, environment setup, custom domains, OAuth)

All documentation is comprehensive and well-organized with clear instructions for both end-users and contributors.

# Rudel

Analytics for [Claude Code](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code). Rudel gives you a dashboard with insights on your coding sessions — token usage, session duration, activity patterns, model usage, and more.

## Prerequisites

- [Bun](https://bun.sh) runtime installed

## Getting Started

1. Create an account at [app.rudel.ai](https://app.rudel.ai)
2. Install the CLI and connect it to your account:

```bash
npm install -g rudel

rudel login     # authenticate via your browser
rudel enable    # auto-upload sessions when Claude Code exits
```

That's it. Your Claude Code sessions will now be uploaded automatically.

Already have past sessions? Upload them in one go:

```bash
rudel upload    # interactive picker for batch upload
```

See the [CLI documentation](apps/cli/README.md) for all available commands.

## How It Works

1. You install the CLI and run `rudel enable`
2. This registers a [Claude Code hook](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks) that runs when a session ends
3. The hook uploads the session transcript to Rudel
4. Transcripts are stored in ClickHouse and processed into analytics

## What Data Is Collected

Each uploaded session includes:

- Session ID & timestamps (start, last interaction)
- User ID & organization ID
- Project path & package name
- Git context (repository, branch, SHA, remote)
- Session transcript (full prompt & response content)
- Sub-agent usage

## Development

See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for local setup, development commands, and PR guidelines.

For self-hosting your own instance, see [docs/self-hosting.md](docs/self-hosting.md).

## Security

To report a vulnerability, see [SECURITY.md](SECURITY.md). Do not open public issues for security concerns.

## License

[MIT](LICENSE)

# Plan: Add org invite step to README Getting Started

## Context

The README's "Getting Started" section covers account creation and CLI setup, but says nothing about inviting teammates. Since sessions are scoped to organizations and the invite flow is non-obvious (link-based, no email), users who want team analytics have no guidance. Adding a brief step makes team onboarding self-service.

## Change

**File:** `/Users/rafa/Obsession/rudel/README.md`

Insert a step 3 after the existing CLI setup steps, before "That's it.":

```
3. Invite teammates (optional): go to **Settings → Organization** in the dashboard, enter their email, and share the generated invite link with them.
```

Keep it to one sentence — no need to explain the link-based mechanism, just tell them where to go and what to do.

## Final README Getting Started section

```markdown
## Getting Started

1. Create an account at [app.rudel.ai](https://app.rudel.ai)
2. Install the CLI and connect it to your account:

```bash
npm install -g rudel

rudel login     # authenticate via your browser
rudel enable    # auto-upload sessions when Claude Code exits
```

3. Invite teammates (optional): go to **Settings → Organization** in the dashboard, enter their email, and share the generated invite link with them.

That's it. Your Claude Code sessions will now be uploaded automatically.
```

## Verification

- Read the updated README visually to confirm the step renders correctly in Markdown
- No code changes, no tests needed

## How It Works

1. You install the CLI and run `rudel enable`
2. This registers a [Claude Code hook](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks) that runs when a session ends
3. The hook uploads the session transcript to Rudel
4. Transcripts are stored in ClickHouse and processed into analytics

## What Data Is Collected

Each uploaded session includes:

- Session ID & timestamps (start, last interaction)
- User ID & organization ID
- Project path & package name
- Git context (repository, branch, SHA, remote)
- Session transcript (full prompt & response content)
- Sub-agent usage

## Development

See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for local setup, development commands, and PR guidelines.

For self-hosting your own instance, see [docs/self-hosting.md](docs/self-hosting.md).

## Security

To report a vulnerability, see [SECURITY.md](SECURITY.md). Do not open public issues for security concerns.
